event: The Kingdom of Urartu emerges around Lake Van, becoming a powerful Iron Age state on the Armenian Highlands.
The Urartian king Argishti I founds the fortress of Erebuni on the site of present-day Yerevan, marking the city's traditional foundation.
Urartu collapses under pressure from the Medes and Scythians, and the region passes into the orbit of incoming peoples.
Armenia is mentioned as a satrapy of the Achaemenid Persian Empire in the Behistun Inscription of Darius I.
The Greek mercenary Xenophon and the Ten Thousand march through Armenia, leaving an early eyewitness account of the land.
After Alexander the Great's victory at Gaugamela, Armenia gains effective independence under the Orontid dynasty.
Following Rome's defeat of the Seleucids at Magnesia, Artaxias I establishes the independent Kingdom of Greater Armenia.
Tigranes the Great ascends the throne and expands Armenia into a short-lived empire stretching from the Caspian to the Mediterranean.
Roman general Lucullus defeats Tigranes the Great and sacks the new capital of Tigranocerta.
Armenia becomes entangled in the Roman-Parthian rivalry as Crassus's defeat at Carrhae reshapes regional power.
Tiridates I is crowned king by Nero in Rome, founding the Arsacid dynasty of Armenia under a Roman-Parthian compromise.
King Tiridates III, converted by Gregory the Illuminator, makes Armenia the first state to adopt Christianity as its official religion.
Mesrop Mashtots creates the Armenian alphabet, enabling a flourishing of native literature and a golden age of letters.
The Arsacid kingdom is abolished and Armenia is partitioned between the Sasanian and Byzantine empires.
Armenian forces under Vardan Mamikonian fight the Sasanians at the Battle of Avarayr to defend their Christian faith.
Arab armies conquer Armenia, which becomes the Umayyad and later Abbasid province of Arminiya under an ostikan governor.
Ashot I is recognized as king, founding the Bagratuni dynasty and restoring an independent Armenian kingdom.
Ani becomes the Bagratuni capital and grows into a celebrated medieval metropolis known as the city of a thousand churches.
The Byzantine Empire annexes the Bagratuni kingdom of Ani, ending Bagratid rule in the heartland.
Seljuk Turks under Alp Arslan capture and sack Ani, accelerating Armenian migration westward.
The Armenian Kingdom of Cilicia is established in the southeast of Asia Minor by refugees and nobles fleeing the Seljuks.
Levon I is crowned king of Cilician Armenia, raising the principality to a full kingdom allied with the Crusaders.
The Mamluks conquer the Kingdom of Cilicia, extinguishing the last independent Armenian state for centuries.
The seat of the Armenian Catholicosate returns to Etchmiadzin, reestablishing it as the spiritual center of the church.
The Peace of Amasya divides Armenia between the Ottoman and Safavid Persian empires.
Shah Abbas I forcibly deports hundreds of thousands of Armenians from the Ararat region to Persia during the scorched-earth campaigns.
The Treaty of Turkmenchay transfers Eastern Armenia, including Yerevan, from Persia to the Russian Empire.
The Hamidian massacres begin under the Ottoman Empire, killing tens of thousands of Armenians.
The Ottoman government launches the Armenian Genocide, deporting and killing an estimated 1.5 million Armenians.
The First Republic of Armenia declares independence on May 28 amid the collapse of the Russian and Ottoman empires.
The Treaty of Sevres recognizes a Wilsonian Armenia, but the Turkish-Armenian War and Soviet invasion end the First Republic.
Armenia is incorporated into the Soviet Union as part of the Transcaucasian Socialist Federative Soviet Republic.
Armenia becomes a separate constituent republic of the USSR as the Armenian Soviet Socialist Republic.
Mass demonstrations in Yerevan mark the fiftieth anniversary of the genocide, reviving Armenian national consciousness.
The Nagorno-Karabakh movement erupts and a devastating earthquake near Spitak kills tens of thousands.
Armenia declares full independence from the collapsing Soviet Union following a popular referendum.
Armenia joins the United Nations as the First Nagorno-Karabakh War with Azerbaijan intensifies.
A ceasefire ends the First Nagorno-Karabakh War, leaving Armenian forces in control of Karabakh and surrounding territory.
Armenia adopts its first post-Soviet constitution, establishing a presidential republic.
Robert Kocharyan becomes president after Levon Ter-Petrosyan resigns over disagreements on the Karabakh peace process.
Gunmen storm the National Assembly and assassinate the prime minister and parliament speaker in a national crisis.
Armenia joins the Council of Europe, deepening ties with European institutions.
Serzh Sargsyan is elected president amid disputed results and deadly post-election clashes in Yerevan.
Armenia joins the Russian-led Eurasian Economic Union and adopts constitutional changes shifting to a parliamentary system.
The peaceful Velvet Revolution led by Nikol Pashinyan ousts the entrenched ruling elite and brings him to power.
The Second Nagorno-Karabakh War ends in defeat, with Azerbaijan retaking much of the disputed territory under a Russian-brokered truce.
Azerbaijan's offensive forces the dissolution of the Nagorno-Karabakh republic and the exodus of nearly the entire Armenian population.
